# Source Card: CALIPER Hematology Reference Standards (I)

## Summary
According to PubMed, this peer-reviewed CALIPER study ([DOI](https://doi.org/10.1093/ajcp/aqaa059), PMID 32561916) establishes age- and sex-specific reference standards for 47 hematologic parameters in 566 healthy children and adolescents (birth to <21 years), all measured on the Beckman Coulter DxH 900 and partitioned per CLSI guidelines. It is a foundational reference-interval source for a pediatric CBC CDS module: it supplies the normative age/sex partitions that a flag/scope-exit converter would consume, while making clear those intervals are analyzer-specific and pediatric (not adult) by design.

## Population / assay / lifecycle notes
- Population: healthy children/adolescents, birth to <21 years, both sexes; CALIPER (Canadian) cohort, n=566. Sex partitioning is material primarily in the 14-<21 y (pubertal/post-pubertal) band.
- Assay/method dependence: every interval is tied to the Beckman Coulter DxH 900. These values are NOT method-agnostic; applying them to other analyzers requires transference/verification per CLSI.
- Lifecycle: published 2020-08; CLSI-based partitioning; positioned as a replacement for using adult intervals in pediatric hematology interpretation.

## Scope-exit / referral relevance (if any)
- The source itself defines reference intervals (2.5th-97.5th percentile normative ranges), not decision/referral thresholds. Any red-flag, scope-exit, or referral trigger is an implementation_proposal to be authored downstream by the CDS converter; this card asserts only the source-supported normative intervals. No diagnosis/treatment/dosing directive is present or implied.

## Limitations & conflicts
- Analyzer specificity (CONFLICT/PORTABILITY): intervals are valid only for the Beckman Coulter DxH 900; other platforms may differ and must not silently reuse these cutoffs.
- Cohort scope: Canadian CALIPER cohort; generalizability to other populations is not established in this paper.
- Neonatal granularity GAP: the youngest partition is 0-<1 y; day-of-life neonatal dynamics (which are steep for HGB/HCT/WBC) are not resolved here — do not infer neonatal-specific cutoffs from this source.
- Full-interval GAP: numeric reference intervals for all 47 parameters reside in Table 1 / supplemental data; only HGB, PLT, and WBC were transcribed in this pass. Remaining per-parameter intervals (e.g., HCT, RBC, MCV, MCH, MCHC, RDW, differential counts, reticulocytes) are "not transcribed in this card" — retrieve from Table 1 before asserting.
- Transcription provenance: Table 1 numeric rows were captured via full-text fetch and cross-checked across two independent reads (agreement on HGB/PLT/WBC); they are not a direct machine-readable export. Verify against the published Table 1 before production CDS use.
- Conflict with adult intervals: the source explicitly argues adult hematology reference intervals are inappropriate for pediatric interpretation — this conflict is preserved, not averaged.
